journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging

title-group.article-title: Combined effect of antioxidant supplementation and resistance training on oxidative stress markers, muscle and body composition in an elderly population

contrib-group.aff: 1Research Center on Aging, University of Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, QC, Canada
2Department of Medicine, University of Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, QC, Canada
3Faculty of Physical Education and Sports, University of Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, QC, Canada
4Research Centre on Aging, 1036 rue Belvédère Sud, J1H 4C4, Sherbrooke, QC, Canada

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: This study was aimed to examine the effect of vitamin C/E intake alone or combined with resistance training on antioxidant/pro-oxidant status, muscle strength and body composition in an elderly population. Fifty-seven men and women with a mean age of 65.6 ± 3.8 years were recruited and randomized in a double-blind fashion into four groups: control-placebo; resistance training (RT); vitamins C/E supplementation (AS); AS+RT. Oxidative stress status and metabolic and lipid profiles were determined at baseline and after six months. Fat-free mass and fat mass measured by DXA were similar at baseline for all groups. At six month, there was a significant difference among the groups as a function of vitamin E supplementation. Moreover, although there was no effect on pro-oxidative parameters, a significant effect on body composition was noted, but no difference was noted on strength gain. The combination of RT+AS had a positive effect on the plasma antioxidant profile but not on the pro-oxidant status.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Aging is associated with biochemical modifications and an increase in free radical production as caused by an alteration of the mitochondrial respiratory chain (1). These age-related changes also lead to a decrease in the functional capacity of the elderly (2) while the imbalance between pro-oxidants and antioxidants leads to an increase in oxidative stress (3) which is believed to be a major contributor to several age-related diseases (4). Since these diseases are a major cause of disability and death (5) in the elderly and may develop into a pandemic in the coming years (6), it is important to develop effective prevention strategies.

Regular physical exercise is critically important for the health and well-being of people of all ages. While an acute exercise session seems to induce oxidative stress (alteration of the balance between pro-oxidant and antioxidant systems) (7) regular exercise training tends to have a beneficial action on this balance (8). However, while some studies have shown that resistance training (RT) may induce a decrease in oxidative stress in younger adults (9), these effects seem to be altered by aging (10). Biochemical changes may explain these discrepancies (11). More specifically, the functionality of the enzymatic system decreases with aging (12). As such, RT coupled with antioxidant supplementation (AS) might be more beneficial than RT alone, especially in elderly populations (13).

Indeed, AS may be an alternative to improving the balance between pro-oxidants and antioxidants and up-regulating enzymatic systems. It has been shown that vitamin C and/or E supplementation, two major antioxidants, can have a positive influence on antioxidant/pro-oxidant status (14). Elderly people are at higher risk of developing oxidative stress conditions due to lower plasma levels of antioxidants, especially vitamins C and E (12). AS has been shown to increase plasma levels of these antioxidants (15) and improve the action of antioxidant enzymes 16, 17. It can also reduce atherosclerosis development by decreasing the susceptibility of lipoproteins to lipid peroxidation 18, 19 and increase the beneficial effects of physical exercise by upregulating angiogenesis and nitric oxide bioavailability (20). These facts support the hypothesis that RT has a more pronounced effect when combined with AS.

Although some studies have investigated the effect of vitamins and exercise in an elderly population, they were limited to acute exercise combined to short supplementation with vitamins. Sacheck et al. used a high dose of vitamin E (1000 IU/d) combined to eccentric exercise and shown that vitamin E supplementation induced modest changes eccentric exercise-induced oxidative stress (21). A study of Latham et al. investigated the effect of vitamin D supplementation on the risk of falling and concluded that vitamin D alone cannot be recommended for use in clinical practice where the primary aim is to improve muscle strength or physical function or reduce the risk of falling in frail older people (22). Evans highlighted the beneficial effect of vitamin E or C towards the reduction of oxidative damage and the skeletal muscle repair and suggested to investigate the effect of combining vitamin E and C on these parameters (23). The group of Fiatarone et al. investigated the effects of macronutrients in frail elderly subjects and concluded that high-intensity resistance exercise training is a feasible and effective means of counteracting muscle weakness and physical frailty in very elderly people (24). In addition to these previous studies, the raised question is whether AS combined with RT in the elderly has a positive effect on the antioxidant/pro-oxidant balance which, when perturbed, can predispose individuals to oxidative damage and oxidative stress-related pathologies, especially the elderly. The aim of our randomized, double-blind, controlled trial was thus to determine whether six months of RT combined with AS would improve the antioxidant/pro-oxidant balance and body composition in a healthy older population.

sec.title: Subjects
sec.p: Fifty-seven subjects (27 men and 30 women) aged between 59 and 73 (65.6 ± 3.8 yrs) were recruited for the study using advertisements in local newspapers. The inclusion criteria were as follows: healthy, no major physical incapacity, sedentary, stable weight (± 2 kg) for the last six months, non-smoker, moderate drinker (maximum 15 g of alcohol/day or the equivalent of one alcoholic beverage/day), no medication that could have an impact on lipid and glucose metabolisms, no menstruations for the preceding 12 months, and no hormonal replacement therapy for women. The sample size was calculated to ensure statistical power when comparing groups (= 0.05; = 0.80), based on an expected difference of 1 kg of muscle mass at the end of the study. This average gain was determined from results obtained in previous studies that have demonstrated such effect 25, 26. In addition, the retention rate observed was 85% while it was expected to be 80%. This rate was based on the study of Lemmer et al. who assessed exercises conducted in older women (26). This sample size would therefore allow to complete the study with a minimum of 16 participants per group.

sec.title: Study procedure
sec.p: A phone interview was conducted to screen for the inclusion criteria. After explaining the nature and goals of the study to the subjects, they were asked to come to the Research Center on Aging (Sherbrooke University Geriatric Institute). Upon arrival, they provided written informed consent. Their resting energy expenditure (REE) was then measured and a 12-hour fasting blood sample was collected. After eating breakfast, their body weight, height, and body composition (DXA) were measured and instructions were provided for keeping their dietary record. The subjects were then asked to complete a three-day dietary record to assess their dietary intakes. At the end of the visit, the subjects were randomized into four groups: (1) placebo, (2) resistance training (RT), (3) vitamin C and E supplementation (AS), and (4) vitamin C and E supplementation combined with resistance training (AS + RT). The double-blind randomization was done by Arkopharma Ltd. The pharmacy of the Sherbrooke University Geriatric Institute provided the vitamin C and E supplements. The subjects were enlisted in this study all over the year. The procedures and protocol were approved by the Ethics Committee of the Sherbrooke University Geriatric Institute. All the following described measurements were performed before and after the intervention period.

sec.title: Antioxidant supplementation
sec.p: The subjects ingested either vitamin C (ascorbate) (1,000 mg; 2 capsules of 500 mg) and E (dl- -tocopherol) (400 IU; 2 capsules of 300 mg) capsules or placebos (lactose: 100 mg) of similar size and appearance daily for six months. The vitamin C capsules were obtained from the Sherbrooke University Geriatric Institute pharmacy (Wampole Brands Inc., Toronto, ON, Canada) while the vitamin E and placebos (for both vitamins) capsules were supplied by Arkopharma Ltd. (Arkopharma Ltd., Carros, France). All capsules (2 capsules) were taken in the morning during breakfast, to avoid loss or omission. All subjects reached compliance minimum participation (95% of the capsules; number of returned capsules and measures of plasma vitamin C and E concentrations) to have their results retained for the study.

sec.title: Resistance training
sec.p: The subjects performed RT three days a week (Monday, Wednesday, and Friday) for six months. Each one-hour session involved warm-up, exercise and recovery phases, and included seven exercises for the upper and lower body (leg press, bench press, leg extension, shoulder press, seated row, triceps extension and biceps curl) with three sets of eight repetitions at 80% of 1-RM (monthly measured and adapted) and abdominal exercise (sit up: 3 x 20 repetitions) was also integrated (27). All subjects reached compliance minimum participation (85% of the sessions) to have their results retained for the study. All sessions were supervised by a kinesiologist.

sec.title: Dietary intake
sec.p: A self administered questionnaire was used to estimate the dietary intakes (28). The subjects were instructed to maintain normal dietary habits throughout the study. They were provided with a five-kg food scale and shown how to complete a three-day dietary record. The results were analyzed using CANDAT SYSTEM version 6.0 software (Candat, London, ON, Canada) to determine daily energy, protein, carbohydrate, and lipid intakes. Protein intakes were subdivided based on the source (animal or vegetable) and lipids were categorized as mono-, poly-, or unsaturated fatty acids.

sec.title: Body composition measurements
sec.p: Determination of fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) was assessed in a supine position, by the use of dual energy Xray absorptiometry (DXA; GE Prodigy Lunar, Madison, WI, USA). In our laboratory, the coefficients of variations for repeated measures of FM and FFM in ten adults (measured one week apart) are 0.4 and 0.5%, respectively. FFM is defined herein as the mass of tissue representing soft tissue exclusively (mineral body mass excluded).

Muscle mass is estimated by urinary excretion of creatinine. Measurement of creatinine excretion is one of the least expensive methods, and more valid and safe for the subjects (29) since creatinine is a degradation product of the phospho-creatinine muscle. The formula reported by Welle et al. (29) was used to calculate the muscle mass: Muscle mass (kg) = {[(Urine quantity (mL) / 100) * (Creatinine quantity (mg/dL))] / 1000} * 21.8.

Visceral FM (VFM) was estimated using the equation developed and validated by Bertin et al. (30): 79.6 x {SD – [(TED - TID) / 2] x TID} / H – 149, where SD is the sagittal diameter (cm), TED is the transverse external diameter (cm), TID is the transverse internal diameter (cm) and H is the height (cm). This estimation of VFM by DXA was correlated [r=0.94 (P<0.0001)] with computed tomographic scan data obtained in overweight women.

sec.title: Blood and urine collection and biochemical analyses
sec.p: Blood samples were collected in the morning after a twelve-hour fasting period and were obtained within the week of end of AS or RT. The plasma lipid profile [high-density lipoprotein (HDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL), total cholesterol, total cholesterol/HDL ratio, triglycerides] was determined in the clinical laboratory of the Centre Hospitalier Universitaire de Sherbrooke as described elsewhere (31). Urine was collected by the participants during three consecutive days. Creatinine was measured by colorimetry as described by Welle et al. (29) (auto-analyzer DCA 2000 +, Bayer, USA).

sec.title: Antioxidant measurements
sec.p: Plasma vitamin C (ascorbate) was analyzed in samples that had been conditioned before freezing. A volume of cold, freshly prepared 10% metaphosphoric acid (100 µL) was added to an equal volume of fresh plasma. The mixture was centrifuged (3,500 x g, 15 min, 4°C) and the supernatant was stored at –80°C. Plasma ascorbate levels were analyzed by HPLC using a CSC Hypersil ODS column (5 µm particles, 25 x 0.43 cm i.d.; Thermo Electron Corporation, MA, USA). A Coulochem II 50-10A electrochemical analytical cell (ESA, MA, USA) was used to measure vitamin C concentrations (32).

sec.title: Vitamin E
sec.p: Plasma vitamin E was analyzed as - and -tocopherol in frozen samples. Briefly, thawed plasma was mixed with an equal volume of ethanol (100 µL). The - and -tocopherol were extracted in hexane (500 µL). Tocopherol acetate was used as an internal standard. Plasma - and -tocopherol were resolved on a Sephasil reverse-phase HPLC column (C18, 5 µm particles, 25 x 0.46 cm i.d.; Pharmacia Biotech, NJ, USA). A Coulochem II 50-10A electrochemical analytical cell (ESA) coupled to a UV photodiode array detector (292 nm, SPD-M10Avp, Shimadzu, MD, USA) was used to measure vitamin E concentrations (18).

sec.title: Total antioxidant status (TAS)
sec.p: TAS levels were determined in frozen plasma using the Trolox equivalent antioxidant assay as described by Miller (33). TAS measures the relative ability of circulating antioxidants to scavenge 2,2’-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) radical cations (ABTSx+) relative to the antioxidant capacity of Trolox standards. The absorbance was measured at 734 nm (U-3000, Hitachi, ON, CA) and the antioxidant activity of the serum was expressed as mmol/L of Trolox equivalent.

sec.title: Albumin and Uric acid
sec.p: Plasma albumin was measured using the bromocresol purple (BCP) dye-binding method as previously described (34;35). In the presence of a solubilizing agent, BCP binds to albumin at pH 4.9. The amount of albumin-BCP complex (absorbance at 600 nm) is directly proportional to the albumin concentration.

Uric acid: Plasma uric acid was measured as previously described (36). Uric acid, which absorbs light at 293 nm, is converted to allantoin by uricase. The change in absorbance at 293 nm due to the disappearance of uric acid is directly proportional to the concentration of uric acid in the sample and is measured using a bichromatic (293, 700 nm) endpoint technique.

sec.title: Residual antioxidant status (RAS)
sec.p: This parameter was determined by subtracting the antioxidant activity attributable to albumin and uric acid from the total antioxidant activity of the plasma samples, which reflects the antioxidant activity of ascorbate, -tocopherol, -carotene, bilirubin, and other radical scavenging antioxidants (33). RAS was determined using the following formula: RAS (mmol/L) = TAS (mmol/L) - [(0.69 x Plasma albumin (mmol/L)) + (Plasma uric acid (mmol/L))] (33). The results are expressed as µM of Trolox equivalent.

sec.title: F2-isoprostane
sec.p: Isoprostanes are prostaglandin-like compounds produced by free radical-mediated lipid peroxidation. Urinary 15-isoprostane F2t (also known as 8-epi-PGF2a, 8-iso-PGF2a or 8-isoprostane) was measured using a competitive enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) (Neogen Corporation, KY, USA) with the absorbance of the reader set at 650 nm. Each sample was analyzed in duplicate (Victor™ X5, PerkinElmer, ON, CA). 15-isoprostane F2t concentrations are expressed as nmol 15-isoprostane F2t/mmol creatinine.

sec.title: Malondialdehyde (MDA)
sec.p: The plasma samples were stored at -80°C until analyzed. Thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances, mainly MDA, were assayed using an ODS Hypersil HPLC column (4.6 x 100 mm, 5 µm, Agilent) and an ODS guard column (5 µm) placed in a column warmer set at 37°C. The HPLC set-up was coupled to a fluorescence detector with an excitation wavelength of 515 nm and an emission wavelength of 553 nm (RF-10Axl, Shimadzu, MD, USA; 515 nm) (37). Malondialdehyde concentrations are expressed as µM.

sec.title: Statistical methods
sec.p: Values are given as mean ± standard deviation (± SD). A one-way ANOVA (with an LSD post-hoc test) was performed to assess differences between 4 groups at baseline. Repeated measure ANOVAs were used to examine the treatment effect on all variables when adjusting for covariates (gender or dietary intake). When the repeated-measure ANOVA was significant, a LSD-multiple comparison post-hoc test was used to locate differences. Independent sample t-tests were used to compare 2 groups at baseline. Paired t-tests were used to assess differences between baseline and post-intervention measurements in each group. The significance was set at P≤0.05. All analyses were performed with the SPSS software version 15.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Baseline characteristics, the antioxidant and pro-oxidant parameters and the physical and biochemical characteristics of the subjects are presented in Table 1, Table 2, Table 3.Table 1

Different from group 1, P≤0.05; b. Different from group 2, P<0.05; c. Different from group 3, P≤0.05; d. Different from group 4, P≤0.05; * Significant treatment effect; P≤0.01; † Significantly different from baseline by paired t-test; P≤0.05; RT = Resistance training; AS = Antioxidant supplementation; FM = Fat mass; VFM = Visceral fat mass; FFM = Fat-free mass; Strength gain (%) = Mean of strength gain in percentage of the 8 exercises; SD = Standard deviation
Data of the first table were similar among groups. Moreover, there was no significant gender-, height-, or weight-based differences among the groups (Table 1).

Significant treatment were noted for vitamin E, these 2 forms (- and -tocopherol) and the ratio (-/ - tocopherol), with significant difference among supplemented groups and the non-supplemented groups (P≤0.01). No treatment effect was noted with the vitamin C, TAS, RAS, MDA and F2-isoprostane measurements, even if slight differences were detected by the paired t-test (Table 2).

Significant treatment were observed for appendicular FFM and muscle mass (P≤0.01), with no difference among groups. No treatment effect was noted with the others parameters, even if slight differences were detected by the paired t-test (Table 3). Finally, no differences were observed for nutritional characteristics of interest at baseline and after the intervention period (data not show).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The purpose of the present study was to determine the impact of a combination of AS and RT on the antioxidant/pro-oxidant profile and the body composition and biochemical parameters of a healthy elderly population.

Plasma vitamin C was within the normal range at baseline for all the groups (20.80 ± 14.41 µM) (38) despite a large dose of vitamin C consumption that exceeded the dietary reference intake (152.2 ± 82.6 vs. 75-90 mg). This low plasma concentration of vitamin C may be explained by a poor intestinal absorption of vitamin C (39). After the intervention, for the supplemented groups, there was a significant increase in the concentration of plasma vitamin C, which reached the normal range (from 23.43 ± 14.40 to 49.66 ± 23.83 µM). It is interesting to note that this concentration of plasma vitamin C is similar to that reported in other studies of elderly populations taking the same daily dose of vitamin C (40;41). Nevertheless, while AS doubled the plasma concentration of vitamin C, it is surprising that the concentration was not higher given the large dose of vitamin C. This strengthens the possibility that the absorption of this compound was limited or impaired in our study groups. Nevertheless, it is important to note that intestinal absorption of ascorbic acid is mediated through a sodium-dependent active transport process I and that high doses of vitamin C are probably not as efficiently absorbed as smaller multiple doses of the vitamin (42). Moreover, the cellular transport of vitamin C is regulated under physiological conditions and altered by aging and disease (43). Lastly, the significant increase in plasma vitamin C in the RT group indicates that RT likely had a specific effect.

Vitamins E and C are important plasma and cellular antioxidants. -tocopherol is the major lipophilic chain-breaking antioxidant. Several studies have shown that these two molecules (α-tocopherol and vitamin C) act in synergy to regulate oxidative stress. At baseline, plasma - and -tocopherol concentrations were in the normal range for all the groups (27.78 ± 5.50 and 3.21 ± 1.23 µM, respectively) 44, 45. After the intervention, for the supplemented groups, there was a significant increase in plasma α-tocopherol. Since dietary intakes did not change (data not shown), the increase in plasma α-tocopherol was most likely due to AS. On the other hand, plasma γ-tocopherol decreased significantly for the intervention groups. This has been attributed to a competition effect between α- and γ-tocopherol for the receptor (46) and to an increase in hepatic turnover of γ-tocopherol following a high intake of α-tocopherol (47). In addition, plasma γ-tocopherol as well as the γ-tocopherol / α-tocopherol ratio have been shown to be possible markers of nutrition-related risk (48). Nevertheless, there is significant inter-individual variability in vitamin E (α- and γ-tocopherol) absorption, which may be caused by intrinsic differences in the expression and activity of intestinal vitamin E (49)

TAS value for our subjects as a whole was 1.12 ± 0.10 mmol/L Trolox equivalents, which is low compared to the normal value reported by Miller and Sung et al. (1.31 to 1.58 mmol/L Trolox equivalents) 33, 38. The TAS value remained low, even when corrected as a function of plasma albumin and uric acid, which is defined as RAS. The RAS values were 0.37 ± 0.09 mmol/L Trolox equivalents, which is low compared to those reported by Miller and Sfrent-Cornateanu et al. (0.45 and 0.92 mmol/L Trolox equivalents, respectively) 33, 50. A number of studies have thus shown that TAS decreases during aging and that there is a gender-related difference, with higher values for men than for women, which is in agreement with our results (data not show). After the interventions, there was no significant difference in TAS or RAS.

The mean MDA value was 1.37 ± 0.37 µM, which is in the normal range for healthy subjects (51). While there was a decrease in MDA concentrations in all the groups after the interventions, the decrease was not statistically significant and may be due to the absence of oxidative stress. These results were confirmed by the levels of urinary F2-isoprostanes, especially 8-iso-prostaglandin F2 (52).

While the improvement in the antioxidant/pro-oxidant profile appeared limited, an impact on body composition was observed. Based on the notion that RT induces oxidative stress and damage, which may prevent adaptations to body composition, AS should reduce oxidative stress and damage and enhance RT-related adaptations. While the only change in body composition was observed with the RT groups (appendicular FFM), the synergy between the two interventions improved body composition at multiple levels. In addition to the gain in FFM, which was also reported by Labonté et al., we observed a significant change in appendicular FFM (53). Despite the fact that the RT groups had a similar antioxidant/pro-oxidant profile before and after the intervention, we only observed significant changes in body composition in the combined intervention group. However, AS did not seem to prevent some of the changes in body composition, especially the decline in muscle mass. Besides, significant differences in FM variables were observed in the placebo group (total FM in kilograms and as a percentage; abdominal FM in kilograms and as a percentage), which may have been due to normal weight gain associated with aging over the six-month period. Because an increase in FM, even limited, is a risk factor for developing some pathologies (CVD, type 2 diabetes, obesity) 54, 55, it is particularly important to protect against FM gains using RT and vitamin E and C supplements. Lastly, even if muscle mass showed a treatment effect, we noted no treatment effect on muscle strength, but only a positive significant effect with similar changes between both groups of RT. Like we found no difference on strength gains between the 2 groups after the intervention, AS did not maximize strength gains during RT. Moreover, we found no association between variations of strength and FFM for total, upper, trunk and lower body segments as muscle strength increased to the same extent while appendicular FFM increased significantly (data not show). This result tends to prove that strength gains occurred by other processes (neurologic (56, 57, 58)) rather than by hypertrophy after 6 months. A 6 month RT program, with or without AS, in healthy older individuals has beneficial effects on strength gains. Compare with current literature, strength gain was of the same magnitude observed after RT program (more than 50%) (59).

The lack of a significant treatment effect may be due to the small size of the cohort, which might have prevented statistical significance from being reached. Like subjects were enlisted in this study all over the year, we estimated that seasonal variations that could be occur were omitted. Thus, the numerous analyses conducted with the small sample may have increased the risks of type I error. Given that the subjects had a relatively normal metabolic profile and antioxidant/pro-oxidant status; it was difficult to detect a beneficial effect on biochemical parameters by our interventions. As dietary intakes did not change during the study (data not shown), this factor could be eliminated as a major cause of changes in parameters, so observed changes could be attributed to the interventions related to the study.

In conclusion, while our results demonstrated that AS combined with RT increases the concentrations of plasma antioxidants that are known to have a beneficial effect on the anti- and pro-oxidant profile in the elderly, the effect did not reach statistical significance on this last status. However, the variations in antioxidant levels might have been responsible for the improvements in FFM and FM. High intensity RT and AS were well-tolerated and should be recommended for other purposes, because elderly seem to maintain a high degree of training capacity. Our study was aimed to examine the combination of these interventions in an elderly population. However, further studies with more subjects will be needed to provide statistically significant results and to be able to propose effective interventions involving RT that consistently provide a beneficial effect.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Acknowledgements: We would like to thank all the subjects who have participated in this study as well as Martine Fisch (R.N.) for her valuable assistance. This study was funded by the Canadian Diabetes Association. The vitamin E and placebos were generously supplied by Arkopharma, Ltd. (Carros, France).
Conflict of Interest: AK received a salary grant from the FRSQ and IJD from the Canadian Institute of Health Research. FB received a D. Menzies bursary.
